flashcards.front: What is the definition of a Binary Search Tree?
flashcards.back: A Binary Search Tree is a binary tree where for each node: all nodes in the left subtree have values less than the node's value, all nodes in the right subtree have values greater than the node's value, and both subtrees are also BSTs.

flashcards.front: What are the three main cases to consider when deleting a node from a BST?
flashcards.back:
1. Deleting a leaf node (no children) - simply remove it
2. Deleting a node with one child - replace the node with its child
3. Deleting a node with two children - replace the node with its inorder successor (smallest in right subtree) or predecessor (largest in left subtree)

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of search, insert, and delete operations in a balanced BST?
flashcards.back: In a balanced BST, all three operations (search, insert, delete) have O(log n) time complexity because the tree height is logarithmic relative to the number of nodes.

flashcards.front: What is the inorder traversal of a BST and what property does it have?
flashcards.back: Inorder traversal of a BST visits nodes in the order: left subtree, root, right subtree. This produces a sorted list of node values in ascending order.

flashcards.front: What happens to the time complexity of BST operations when the tree becomes unbalanced?
flashcards.back: When a BST becomes unbalanced (essentially a linked list), the time complexity of search, insert, and delete operations degrades to O(n) because we may need to traverse all nodes in the worst case.
